topic: Who's Actually Paying for Your Cash Back
threads: The new Visa/Mastercard settlement lets merchants surcharge or decline by card tier for the first time. Once that plays out at real registers, does the cross-subsidy actually shrink — or just relocate?

The 2010/2013 dispute was fought entirely with assumption-sensitive models that can't agree on the transfer's sign. Is there a way to estimate it directly from real transaction and pricing data instead?

Australia (cap the fee, ban the surcharge) and the U.S. (legalize the surcharge, leave the fee alone) are running opposite bets on the same problem starting the same year. Which actually shrinks the transfer more — and does either one do anything for the unbanked households sitting outside the mechanism entirely?

topic: The Reef With an Insurance Policy
threads: Has anyone retuned the Puerto Morelos-specific payout tiers against brigade throughput, the way WTW reportedly did for the Belize sister-policy after Hurricane Lisa — or is the original 2018/2019 wind-mortality calibration still setting today's thresholds?

Did Hurricane Grace cause reef damage in 2021 that went uncompensated? A post-storm damage assessment checked against the wind-speed record could actually settle whether that non-payout was basis risk or correct calibration — nobody seems to have run it yet.

The Maldives sea-surface-temperature pilot is the first real attempt at a "dosimeter" trigger for reefs. Can a cumulative-exposure instrument pay out fast and cheap enough to function the way the wind trigger does, or does insuring a slope instead of a spike break the economics that make parametric coral insurance work at all?

Whether Olga Jonas's account of internal Bank knowledge can be corroborated — an internal memo, a second insider, anything that would settle whether the triggers' weakness was foreseen at design time in 2016–17 or only recognized once the bonds began failing to pay.

Whether the "whoever sets the dial pays only for false positives" pattern shows up in other financialized duty-of-care instruments — catastrophe bonds for hurricanes, sovereign disaster insurance, pandemic-adjacent parametric products — or whether pandemic bonds are closer to a one-off design failure than a template.

How the 2026 Bundibugyo response actually finishes: whether the Pandemic Fund's frontline disbursement caught up after the Independent Panel's early criticism, and what a full accounting of that outbreak looks like next to Kasese and the 2018–20 Kivu epidemic.
